exercises.title: Identity and first commit
exercises.prompt: Configure your git identity, initialize a fresh repository, and make your first commit containing a single `README.md`. Confirm the commit landed with the log.
exercises.hint: Set `user.name` and `user.email` globally, then the add → commit → log cycle.
exercises.solution:
```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"

mkdir my-lab && cd my-lab
git init
echo "# My AI Lab" > README.md
git add README.md
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git log --oneline   # shows your one commit
```

exercises.title: Branch, experiment, merge
exercises.prompt: From `main`, create a branch called `experiment`, make a change there, commit it, then merge it back into `main` — without ever committing the experiment directly on `main`. Show the history proving the branch was merged.
exercises.hint: `git checkout -b` creates and switches; switch back to `main` before merging.
exercises.solution:
```bash
git checkout -b experiment
echo "print('hello')" > train.py
git add train.py && git commit -m "Add training stub"

git checkout main
git merge experiment
git log --oneline --graph   # shows the merge
```

Branching is how you try a risky idea (a new architecture, a refactor) without endangering known-good code on `main`. If the experiment fails, you just delete the branch (`git branch -D experiment`).

exercises.title: A .gitignore that fits AI work
exercises.prompt: Write a `.gitignore` appropriate for an AI project, then prove it works: create a fake 500 MB checkpoint and a virtual-env directory and show that `git status` does not list them. Explain why committing those would be a problem.
exercises.hint: Model weights and environments are large and regenerable — they don't belong in version control. Patterns like `*.ckpt`, `*.pt`, `.venv/`.
exercises.solution:
```bash
cat > .gitignore <<'EOF'
# environments
.venv/
__pycache__/
# model checkpoints / weights
*.ckpt
*.pt
*.safetensors
# data
data/
*.csv
# notebooks scratch
.ipynb_checkpoints/
EOF

mkdir -p .venv && dd if=/dev/zero of=model.ckpt bs=1M count=500 2>/dev/null
git status            # model.ckpt and .venv/ are NOT listed
git add .gitignore && git commit -m "Add .gitignore"
```

Git stores every version of every file forever. A single committed 500 MB checkpoint bloats the repo permanently — even deleting it later leaves it in history. Keep weights in object storage or a model registry, not git.
